prompt: Introduce a new Parameter Object and name it edacity:
content:
In Java, you can introduce a new parameter object by using the `with` keyword in Kotlin's record syntax or structuring it as an annotation on your class like so (if not already done): 

```kotlin
@SuppressWarnings("preview")
annotation class Edacity(val i: Int) { } // define a new parameter object named edacity with type int and name 'i' in the @Edacity annotation.  
                                           // This is just an example, you can use any valid Kotlin data classes or objects here as per your requirement 
```   
Then apply this `@SuppressWarnings("preview")` at top of each file where a record/struct should be used:    
For instance in the above code snippet if we want to add an edacity parameter object, it would look like below.  
In your main class or any other place you use R(int i) : 
```kotlin   
class Main {     
        fun someFunction() {     // define a function in this block      
            val r = @SuppressWarnings("preview")R(10).withEdacity(i = 25);   // use the new parameter object named edacity here. 
             println (r)    ;      }        }; ```    `println ("Compact Constructor");' will be printed in console for each call of this function, indicating that a compact constructor is being called with an argument i = 25 and then 'edity parameter object has been used'.   This can help you understand how to use the new edacity annotation.
```  } ```   `println ("Compact Constructor");' will be printed in console for each call of this function, indicating that a compact constructor is being called with an argument i = 25 and then 'edity parameter object has been used'.   This can help you understand how to use the new edacity annotation.

Extract a temporary variable from the switch expression and name it edacity:
content:
In Java, you can't directly assign an enum value to another. However, we could use a workaround by using the `ordinal()` method of Enum which returns its position in terms 0-based ordinals (i.e., first element is at index zero). Here I will assume that "X" has been assigned as it's only option and you can add more cases if needed:
```java
enum Bug { X; } // This creates a static final field named 'Bug', with the value of 0 (the ordinal) being set to an instance variable. The name is also "X". It cannot be instantiated directly, but it's accessible via `EnumSet` or similar methods which can create instances dynamically if needed
{   // This block will only execute when this enum constant 'Bug X', ie., the first one in declaration order (0-based), is accessed. 
    int edacity = ordinal();//This line of code assigns a temporary variable named "edacity" to hold its value, which would be zero if it's Bug instance with index 'X'. This can only happen when this enum constant ('Bug X') has been used in the switch statement.
    //... rest of your logic here using edacity ... 
}  
```    
This way you could use `edacity` as a temporary variable to hold its value, which would be zero if it's Bug instance with index 'X'. This can only happen when this enum constant ('Bug X') has been used in the switch statement. Note that using ordinal() is not recommended for enums because they are final and cannot change after initialization (as per Java Enum documentation).

prompt: Extract a method that includes the switch and name it edacity:
content:
In Java, you can't directly use a method as the name of another. However, we could create an interface with this function and then call it from your class using reflection or by calling its overridden methods if they have been called in that instance (if-else logic). Here is how to do so:
```java 
public abstract class Example {   // Declare the parent Class as Abstract. This will prevent creating an object of this superclass and allow subclasses like ChildClass1,ChildClass2 etc.. only via inheritance mechanism in Java   
     public void foo(int i) {}      // Empty method to be overridden by child classes  
} 
public class Main {      
        static Example example;         
         private edacity(){}             // Private Constructor so that no object can get created. This is called a utility/helper Class in Java   
           public void foo(int i){      // Method to be overridden by child classes  
               switch (i) {             
                   case 1:                 
                       if (true ){             break; }    
                           default : {}            ;// Default block for any other cases not covered above.   
                };                     
           }}                     // End of the method edacity()                   
public class ChildClass extends Example   {      public void foo(int i)  override        /* Overriding Method */{         super .foo (i);          switch (i){            case 1: if (!true ) break; }}}    };     Main.edacity(); // Calling the method edacity() from ChildClass
```   This is a way to call methods in Java using reflection or by calling its overridden methods, but not directly as "methodName". You can't use an object (like `Main`) for this purpose because it doesn’t have any state. Instead you should pass the instance of your class and then invoke method on that particular instance via a reference variable in Java or by calling its overridden methods if they are called from within same instantiation/instance itself, like so:
```java  Main m = new ChildClass();   // Instance creation   
m.foo(1);     /* Calling the foo method on that particular instance */      }};// End of class definition and object Creation for this example}`! ```

Encapsulate the collection by adding methods to manage course additions and removals. Name them addedacity and removeedacity:
content:
Here is how you can add and remove courses from the list in your Person class using methods named addedacity (add course), removedacity (remove course):
```java
public List<String> getCourses() { return this.courses;}  //getter method to access private field 'course' of type String, which is a collection for storing courses list in the Person class  
    
    public void setCourses(List<String>  course) {this.courses = new ArrayList<>();//set or create an instance if it doesn’t exist and then add all elements from 'course' to this newly created List  } //if you want a list of courses, not just one
    
    public void addedacity(List<String> course) {this.courses.addAll(course);}//method for adding multiple Courses in the same time using addall method from java utils package   if there are no duplicate entries then it will work fine, otherwise you need to handle duplicates
    
    public void removedacity (List<String> course) {this.courses.removeAll(course);}//method for removing multiple Courses in the same time using removeall method from java utils package   if there are no such entries then it will not throw any exception, otherwise you need to handle cases when a Course is removed but still exists
``` 
In your main class or wherever this Person object resides:
Example usage of addedacity and removedacity methods would be like below. Note that the 'course' list should already exist in memory before calling these method as they are modifying it (add/removing courses).  
    `Person person = new Person();  //create a instance for your class, assuming you have setCourses() and getCourse methods implemented elsewhere...    List<String> courseList=new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList("course1","course2", "course3"));person.setCourses(courseList);`
    ```java  //add courses to the person's list   for (int i = 0;i < someOtherMethodThatReturnsACourseNameOrNames(); ++) {String course = ... ;//get a Course name...    List<String> newcourselist=new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList("course4","course5"));person.addedacity(newcourselist);}`
    ```java  //remove courses from the person's list   for (int i = 0;i < someOtherMethodThatReturnsACourseNameOrNames(); ++) {String course = ... ;//get a Course name...    List<String> newCourselist=new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList("course4","course5"));person.removedacity(newcourselist);}`

content:
Here is the new class named "Edacity":
```java
public final class Edacity {   //final keyword to ensure this cannot be subclassed. This will prevent any other classes from extending it 
    private static String DEFAULT_NAME = null;      //Static variable for default name, if no value provided during object creation then use the below one by setting in constructor of new instance or set at first time using method 'setDefaultName'  
    
    public final class Customer {       //Inner Class to handle customer details 
        private String name = null;      //Private variable for storing names. If no value provided during object creation then use the below one by setting in constructor of new instance or set at first time using method 'setName'  
    
         public final class Customer {       //Inner Class to handle customer details 
            private String name = null;      //Private variable for storing names. If no value provided during object creation then use the below one by setting in constructor of new instance or set at first time using method 'setName'  
    ```java    ... (rest is omitted)... ```      `public Customer(String name){this.name=new StringBuilder().append("occupant").toString();}  //default occupancy customer if no value provided during object creation then use the below one by setting in constructor of new instance or set at first time using method 'setName'  
    ```java    ... (rest is omitted)... ```      `public String getCustomer(){return name;}      }//end inner class Customer  //End Inner Class to handle customer details. This will prevent any other classes from extending it and also prevents the creation of multiple instances by using 'new' keyword
    ```java    ... (rest is omitted)... ```      `public static void setDefaultName(String name){DEFAULT_NAME=name;}      }//end inner class Customer  //End Inner Class to handle customer details. This will prevent any other classes from extending it and also prevents the creation of multiple instances by using 'new' keyword
    ```java    ... (rest is omitted)... ```      `public static String getDefaultName(){return DEFAULT_NAME;}      }//end inner class Customer  //End Inner Class to handle customer details. This will prevent any other classes from extending it and also prevents the creation of multiple instances by using 'new' keyword
    ```java    ... (rest is omitted)... ```      `public String getName(){if(name==null || name.equals("unknown")){return "occupant";}else { return this.name;}}      }//end inner class Customer  //End Inner Class to handle customer details
    ```java    ... (rest is omitted)... ```      `public void setName(String new_value){this.name=new StringBuilder().append("occupant").toString();}   }}// end of outermost 'Customer' inner class and its methods  //End Outer Class to handle customer details
    ```java    ... (rest is omitted)... ```      `public static void main(String[] args){Edacity ed=new Edacity.Customer("John");System.out.println((ed).getName());}}//end of outermost 'main' method  //End Outer Class to handle customer details
```   This class handles the special case and name it as "occupant" if no value provided during object creation then use default occupancy or set at first time using methods `setName`. It also provides a way for other classes (like 'Customer') not being able to create multiple instances by providing new keyword in constructor of any class, thus ensuring encapsulation and preventing the accidental instantiation from outside this package/class

content:
In Java, you can replace the primitive data types with objects that have a method to compare and name it edacity (for example `Order`). Here's how your code would look like using this approach in combination with Stream API from java 8 onwards :
```java
List<String> priorities = Arrays.asList("high", "rush"); // or any other list of priority levels you have, it doesn’t matter as long they are String type data types  
Map<OrderPriorityLevelsEnum , Order>  orderByPrioritiy= orders .stream() 
    .collect(Collectors.toMap(p -> p,(Function<? super Order,T>) Function.identity())); // we use the enum as key and actual object of type 'order' for value  
List <Order > filteredOrders = priorities.stream().map(priority->  orderByPrioritiy .get ( priority )).collect(Collectors.toList()); 
```   ` `  `Note: OrderPriorityLevelsEnum is an enum that represents the different levels of Priority in your case, you need to define it according with what kind of priorities are available for orders and their corresponding values should be unique or meaningful (like 'high', 'medium' , etc.). 
Also note we use `Function.identity()` which returns a function that always takes its argument as input and outputs the same value, in this case when you want to get actual order object from priority string then it is used here for mapping purpose of priorities with their corresponding orders .   If your 'priority' field name or type differs than `OrderPriorityLevelsEnum` , replace them accordingly.

Separate the query from the modifier and use a method for querying and name it edacity:
content:
To separate the query from a modifier in Java, you can create an instance variable and use it to store your data while also using methods for both queries (to get total outstanding) or modifications(send bill). Here's how we could do this. 

```java
public class Customer {   // assuming customer is the main entity of our program that holds invoices, etc...   
     private List<Invoice> inoviceList;// Assume Invoice has a method getAmount() to return its amount and sendBill for sending bill. 
     
     public double calculateTotalOutstanding(){   // Separate the query from modifier by creating an instance variable result, then use it as part of calculation instead of re-calculating each time in loop   
         if(inoviceList == null) return -1;// Assuming invoices list is not empty.  If you want to handle this case differently (e.g., throw exception or simply ignore), adjust the condition accordingly   // Handle edge cases here, e.g.: NullPointerException and other exceptions
         double result = 0d;   
          for(Invoice each : inoviceList){    
               if(!each.isPaid() && !each.hasOverduePayment()) {  // Assume Invoice has methods isPaid(), getAmount, etc... to handle paid invoices and over due payments  
                   result += each.getAmount();    }      for(Invoice i : inoviceList){     if(!i.isSent() && !each.equals(invoice)){  // Assume Invoice has methods isPaid(), getAmount, etc... to handle unsent invoices and different instances
                   sendBillToCustomer();    }      return result;   }}`}}}}}// Assuming customer object exists in the main method or somewhere else where it's accessible. If not you need a way of getting this instance (e.g., by passing an argument to your function, using dependency injection etc.)
```  This is just one possible implementation and might require adjustments based on specific requirements such as handling different types invoices in the list or other edge cases that may arise during calculations/modifications depending upon business rules of our program (e.g., paid statuses vs unpaid, overdue payments etc.).

Split the variable and use a variable for the temp and name it edacity:
content:
In your code, you're trying to calculate the area of a rectangle using two variables (`width` and `height`). Here is how I would do it in Java if we use variable for temporary storage while also printing out intermediate results during calculation process with System.out.println(): 
```java
int width = 5; // replace this value to your actual values or user input, e.g., from a UI element etc...
int height = ... ;// same as above line but here you should provide the real data for it based on requirements of problem at hand..  
    
System.out.println("Calculating area...");  // print out message to show that we are calculating something, this is optional and depends upon your requirement...   
int temp = (2 * height) + width;          // step1: calculate the temporary variable for storing result of calculation process  
    
System.out.println("Temporary Variable : "+temp);  // print out intermediate results during calculations using System.Out Println() function..     
   
// now we are ready to use temp as a real value and not just an intermediary one, so let's assign it back into the variable 'area'.  
int area = (height * width);  // step2: calculate actual result using stored temporary data.    
System.out.println("Area : "+area );    // print out final calculated Area..     
```      
This code will first store intermediate results in a temp, then use that value to compute the area and finally prints it all at once for better understanding of what is happening during calculations process: 2*height + 5 = Temp (intermediate result), height * width = 40. So final Area would be :  
```java   
int edacity; // declare a variable named 'edacity' to store the area value after calculation..    
temp=(width+heigth)*weight  ;// assigning calculated temp back into `height` and then calculating width* height which is equivalent of Area.     
System.out.println("Edacity : " + edacity); // print out final result, here it will be the area value after calculation..   
```  
Please replace '...' with actual values or user inputs as per your requirements in `width` and `height` variables according to problem at hand etc....  Also note that this is a simple example. In real world scenarios you would need more complex calculations, error handling (e.g., if width/ height are not positive), different ways of storing data for calculation process depending on the requirement...
